text: Use a unique passphrase for every important account and a password manager to remember them; reused passwords are the top cause of account takeovers.
Turn on multi-factor authentication wherever it's offered; it blocks the vast majority of automated account-compromise attempts.
Keep software and your phone OS updated - most exploited vulnerabilities already have a patch available when attacks happen.
A padlock icon means traffic is encrypted (HTTPS), not that a site is trustworthy; check the domain name itself before entering data.
Back up important data on the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, on 2 types of media, with 1 kept off-site or offline.
App permissions are opt-in trust: an offline flashlight app has no reason to need your contacts or location.
Public Wi-Fi is shared; avoid logging into banking on it, or use a trusted VPN, since others on the network can attempt to intercept traffic.
Phishing relies on urgency - 'act now or lose access.' Slow down and verify the sender through a known channel before clicking.
Longer passwords beat complex short ones: a 16-character passphrase is far harder to crack than 'P@ss1!'.
'Free' apps often pay for themselves with your data; check what a service collects and why before signing up.
Enable automatic screen lock and device encryption on phones and laptops so a lost device doesn't expose your accounts.
QR codes can hide malicious links; treat a scanned URL with the same caution as one in an unexpected email.
